coredata.dc:title: A dual-functional paper-based analytical device for ultrasensitive detection of peanut allergen-specific IgE
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Ze-Nan
Ding, Jun-Jie
Shi, Xin-Qiao
Yuan, Ying
Wang, Meng-Tian
Yu, Li-Na
Wang, Xiao-Jun
Shen, Peng
coredata.dc:description:
Background Increasing attention has been caught by the allergy-related food safety issue. The rapid and sensitive diagnosing approaches are still in high demand for providing clinical reference. Paper-based analytical devices (PADs) are appealing candidates for allergy diagnosis and prediction due to their portability, stability, and operational easiness. However, the sensitivity of PADs needs to be further improved for the targets with low abundance. In addition to the complex signal amplifications, an alternative strategy that requires fewer reagents, steps, and shorter time is anticipated. (82)
Results We report fluorescent PADs (FPADs) that can accumulate and detect the major peanut allergen glycoprotein Arachis hypogaea h2 (Ara h2)-specific IgE (sIgE). The FPADs are constructed by in-situ synthesis of blue-emissive carbon dots (BCDs) on the surface of cellulose paper, followed by the conjugation of Ara h2. After the capture of sIgE, a green-emissive carbon dots-labeled secondary anti-sIgE reporter (Ab2-GCDs) is assembled on FPADs. The detection relies on the sIgE concentration-dependent color variation of FPADs. In addition, the accumulation of sIgE is achievable by filtering the sample through FPADs, improving the assay sensitivity and efficiency. It is demonstrated that the limit of detection (LOD) is 15.7 ng/mL, evidently lower than the simple immersion-based assay (90.2 ng/mL). The excellent selectivity allows sIgE quantification in serum with high accuracy. (130)
Significance By harnessing the outperforming sensing performance of the proposed FPADs, the rapid, accurate, and cost-efficient diagnosis and prediction of peanut allergy can be realized. In addition, the FPADs could serve as a universal sensing platform for varying targets by flexibly engineering the capture moieties on the surface of fluorescent paper. (50)
coredata.dcterms:subject: Allergy
Ara h2
Fluorescent paper-based analytical device
Filtration
Detection
Peanut

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of BCDs on filter paper and GCDs in solution
Preparations of FPAD and Ab2-GCDs reporter
Facile synthesis of the spectrally-resolved BCDs and GCDs
Bio-functionalization of the fluorescent paper and GCDs
Efficient and sensitive sIgE detection by harnessing the bi-functional FPADs
Detection of sIgE in practical samples with excellent selectivity and accuracy

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the important financial support from Suzhou Wuzhong People's Hospital.
The authors acknowledge the important financial support from Suzhou Wuzhong People\u2019s Hospital.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of defiberized cellulose from the fluorescent paper (i), high-resolution TEM image of BCDs in-situ synthesized on paper (ii), and defiberized cellulose from the untreated paper substrate. (B) TEM image (i), high-resolution TEM image (ii), and size distribution histograms (iii) of the GCDs. (C) Excitation-emission mapping of the fluorescent paper (i) and GCDs (ii).

(A) EDX mapping of C, O, N, and S for the samples of cellulose paper (i), fluorescent paper with BCDs (ii), and Ara h2 functionalized fluorescent paper (iii). (B) FT-IR spectra of Ab2 (i), GCDs (ii), and Ab2-GCDs (iii).

Fluorescent images (repeated 3 times) and the relative green-to-blue emission intensity plots for the FPADs in the presence of varying concentrations of sIgE. (A) and (B) represented the filtration-based assay and immersion-based assay, respectively. Letters on the plots indicated the results of difference analysis. The difference is insignificant for the same letter labeled plots. p-value threshold: 0.01. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescent images and the corresponding green-to-blue fluorescence ratio of FPADs treated by sIgE and varying substances in blood including NaCl (200 mM), glucose (Gluc, 10 mM), histine (3 mM), glutamic acid (GA, 3 mM), and IgG (10 mg/mL), as well as sIgE coexisted with the substances. Letters on the bars indicated the significance of the difference. p-value threshold: 0.01. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Assay of sIgE in simulated practical samples spiked with different concentrations of the target.

(A) Illustration of the stepwise modification of cellulose paper for bi-functional FPADs construction. CA was modified onto the cellulose fibers by esterification, followed by the in-situ synthesis of BCDs via carbonization of CA. Ara h2 was conjugated for capturing sIgE. (B) Filtration-based target binding and fluorescent labeling to generate the fluorescent signal. (C) Schematic for the smartphone-assisted quick analysis of sIgE.
Detected sIgE concentrations based on FPADs and testing kits in practical samples.

head.ce:title: A dual-functional paper-based analytical device for ultrasensitive detection of peanut allergen-specific IgE

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Suzhou Wuzhong People's Hospital, Suzhou, Jiangsu, 215128, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Suzhou Wuzhong People’s Hospital, Suzhou, Jiangsu, 215128, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Sunlant Bioengineering Co., Ltd, Wuxi, Jiangsu, 214038, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Sunlant Bioengineering Co., Ltd, Wuxi, Jiangsu, 214038, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Increasing attention has been caught by the allergy-related food safety issue. The rapid and sensitive diagnosing approaches are still in high demand for providing clinical reference. Paper-based analytical devices (PADs) are appealing candidates for allergy diagnosis and prediction due to their portability, stability, and operational easiness. However, the sensitivity of PADs needs to be further improved for the targets with low abundance. In addition to the complex signal amplifications, an alternative strategy that requires fewer reagents, steps, and shorter time is anticipated. (82)

We report fluorescent PADs (FPADs) that can accumulate and detect the major peanut allergen glycoprotein Arachis hypogaea h2 (Ara h2)-specific IgE (sIgE). The FPADs are constructed by in-situ synthesis of blue-emissive carbon dots (BCDs) on the surface of cellulose paper, followed by the conjugation of Ara h2. After the capture of sIgE, a green-emissive carbon dots-labeled secondary anti-sIgE reporter (Ab2-GCDs) is assembled on FPADs. The detection relies on the sIgE concentration-dependent color variation of FPADs. In addition, the accumulation of sIgE is achievable by filtering the sample through FPADs, improving the assay sensitivity and efficiency. It is demonstrated that the limit of detection (LOD) is 15.7 ng/mL, evidently lower than the simple immersion-based assay (90.2 ng/mL). The excellent selectivity allows sIgE quantification in serum with high accuracy. (130)

By harnessing the outperforming sensing performance of the proposed FPADs, the rapid, accurate, and cost-efficient diagnosis and prediction of peanut allergy can be realized. In addition, the FPADs could serve as a universal sensing platform for varying targets by flexibly engineering the capture moieties on the surface of fluorescent paper. (50)

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food allergy indicates the abnormally strong immune responses caused by the allergens in food, which can be categorized as specific IgE (sIgE) mediated allergy and non-IgE mediated allergy [1]. The former occurred more commonly following the cascade reactions of allergen-sIgE complex formation, immune cell sensitization, active medium (histamine, 5-hydroxytryptamine, leukotrienes, etc.) release, and finally the occurrence of local or systematic allergic reactions [2]. According to the FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations), peanuts belong to the allergic food that can result in the most severe immune reactions. Due to the severity and persistence of peanut allergy, trace amounts of peanut protein can cause serious reactions in peanut-allergic patients, greatly damaging their life safety and quality of life [3–5]. Therefore, the detection and sensitization evaluation of peanut allergens is of great significance in controlling the risk of peanut allergy. In the allergic protein family of peanuts, Ara h2 is one of the most frequently detected proteins that possess higher tolerance to protease than its homologies (Ara h1 and Ara h3) [6]. It has been evidenced that Ara h2 is rich in disulfide bonds, leading to its stability and the persistence of immune reaction [7].

With the continuous development of allergen detection techniques, a plethora of assay platforms have been proposed for the allergic proteins in peanuts. According to the type of receptors, these assays can be classified as protein-based and nucleic acid-based [8–12]. The protein-based assays are represented by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [13,14], immunochromatographic assay [15,16], and Immunoimprinting assay [17,18]. Despite the great progress that has been made, the detection of allergens in food is useful for routine food safety assessment, but incompetent for confirming clinically relevant sensitization and predicting the severity after peanut ingestion. Alternatively, the quantification of sIgE for Ara h2 has been paid more attention in comparison to the allergen detection. For instance, Joshi et al. reported a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) imaging microarray for quantifying sIgE in serum [19]. The Ara h2 was fixed on a gold SPR sensor slide, followed by the binding of sIgE/Ab2/magnetic bead. The SPR signals were imaged for sIgE quantification. Shen et al. further differentiated IgE antibodies by epitope-resolved detection [20]. Differential epitope responses to allergen-specific IgE were achieved by providing a binding environment designed to detect low IgE concentrations with minimal cross-reactivity.

Paper-based analytical devices (PADs) are well-known for their advantages of portability, long-term stability, and cost efficiency, making them promising in point-of-care testing (POCT) applications [21–23]. The cellulose substrate is rich in hydroxyl groups that allow flexible conjugations of sensing units. The capillary effect provided by the fiber matrix permits efficient sensing reactions that lead to the signal output. Besides, the processing easiness of paper encouraged researchers to exploit PADs in diverse formats such as lateral flow assay devices [24–26], microfluidic, and 3D microfluidic devices [27–31]. For the sake of expanding the applications of PADs in the sensing of hazardous substances or biomarkers that are in extremely low abundance, their sensitivity enhancement was attempted by several strategies. In the colorimetric PADs, the gold-label silver-stain technique was proved to be effective for signal enhancement [32,33]. For the PADs integrated with biomolecules, the signals were boosted by the cascade enzymatic catalysis [34], DNA amplification (loop-mediated isothermal amplification [35], rolling circle amplification [36], etc.), or DNA assembly (strand displacement reaction [37], hybridization chain reaction [38], entropy-driven DNA assembly [39]). Nevertheless, the signal amplification by additional chemical/biological reactions largely increased the cost and complexity of the sensing system. Another evident advantage of the PADs is their filter function which was previously ignored by the researchers. The extremely diluted target in a homogenous solution is considered to be effectively accumulated on the PADs by filtration, realizing the ultrasensitive detection that is hardly achievable in solution.

Herein, to accurately quantify the sIgE responsive to Ara h2 and evaluate the potential of peanut allergy in a person, we report a bifunctional fluorescent paper-based analytical device (FPAD) that can accumulate the target by filtration and generate the fluorescent signal after labeling. As shown in Scheme 1 , fluorescent functionalization of filter paper substrates is performed by the in-situ synthesis of blue-emissive carbon dots (BCDs). Subsequently, the Ara h2 molecules were conjugated on the fluorescent paper for capturing sIgE. The fluorescent reporters, which are composed of anti-sIgE molecules and green-emissive carbon dots (Ab2-GCDs), are used to label the sIgE. The relative green-to-blue fluorescent stability is a function of the sIgE amount, giving rise to the quantification of sIgE with a spectrophotometer. Moreover, the ratiometric fluorescent response leads to the color change of the FPAD, enabling the sIgE abundance estimation with a hand-held UV lamp and a smartphone. In combination with its excellent stability, the FPAD is believed to be a promising clinical tool for peanut allergy diagnosis and evaluation.

Citric acid (CA, 99 %), ethanediamine (EDA, 99.5 %), and urea (99 %) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Filter paper (Whatman, Grade 42) was received from Focmcs (Shanghai, China). 1-(3-(Dimethylamino)propyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 99 %), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 97 %), bovine serum albumin (BSA, 98 %), and monoclonal Anti-Human IgE (Ab2) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. Peanut allergen Ara h2 antigen was supplied by Anti Biotech. Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). Monoclonal sIgE (1C4 anti-Ara h2, produced in ascites and purified by affinity chromatography) was obtained from Wolcavi Biotech. (Beijing, China). Mouse peanut sIgE (MP-sIgE) ELISA kit was purchased from Zhenke Biotech. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were used as received and no additional purification was performed. Pure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained from a Pall Cascade AN system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BCDs on filter paper and GCDs in solution
ce:section.ce:para: For synthesizing BCDs on the paper substrate, CA (carbon source) was first modified onto the paper via esterification. In detail, 1 g of the paper was immersed into 15 mL of CA solution (50 wt%, containing 3 wt% NaH2PO2 as the catalyst), followed by heating the mixture to 80 °C in a water bath. After 2 h, the CA-modified filter paper, EDA (0.3 g), 25 mL of pure water, and 12.5 mL of ethanol were transferred to a 50 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon) liner stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 180 °C for 6 h. The BCDs decorated filter paper was purified by washing it in pure water until a non-fluorescent supernatant was obtained. The fluorescent paper was naturally dried for further use. The GCDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of CA and urea. 3 g of CA and 1.8 g of urea were dissolved in 25 mL of water, which was transferred to a one-neck flask (100 mL). The flask was placed in a microwave reactor and heated to 180 °C at a power density of 600 W. The heating lasted for 10 min before the addition of 25 mL of cold water. The carbonization product was purified by filtrating through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, followed by dialyzing for 48 h in a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1 kDa). The GCDs were stored as powder after the purified dispersion was dried by rotary evaporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations of FPAD and Ab2-GCDs reporter
ce:section.ce:para: The FPAD was constructed by conjugating Ara h2 on the surface of a fluorescent paper via a carbodiimide reaction. As a typical procedure, the carboxyl groups on paper sheets were activated by EDC (5 wt%) and stabilized by NHS (5 wt%) at room temperature for 2 h in the 2-morpholinoethanesulphonic acid (MES) buffer (100 mM, pH 5.0). A stock solution of Ara h2 was added to the activated paper sheets. The final concentration of Ara h2 was 0.25 mg/mL. After shaking for 4 h, the FPAD was washed with PBS buffer (100 mM, pH 7.4) for three times and blocked with BSA (0.5 mg/mL). The size of FPADs was standardized by cutting Ara h2-modified fluorescent paper into round-shaped sheets with a diameter of 6 mm. To compare the analytical performances of FPADs to those without the blue emission background, the cellulose paper modified by citric acid but not experienced the carbonization procedure was also used as the Ara h2-loading substrate. Modification of Ara h2 on the non-fluorescent cellulose paper followed consistent protocol with that of fluorescent paper.

The Ab2-GCDs reporter was also prepared via the carbodiimide reaction. In detail, 0.2 mg of GCDs powder was added to 200 μL of Ab2 solution with a concentration of 5 mg/mL. After the absolute dispersion of GCDs, 1 mg of EDC and 1 mg of NHS were added to the solution. The conjugation was proceeded by overnight shaking. For removing the excessive GCDs, EDC, as well as NHS, the mixture was ultrafiltered in a millipore microtube (MWCO = 10 kDa) and washed with PBS buffer for 3 times. The purified Ab2-GCDs fluorescent reporter was dissolved in PBS buffer (100 mM, pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C for future use.

Being served as a filter paper and sensing device, the FPAD was fixed in a customized strainer. 0.5 mL of the samples with varying concentrations of sIgE were loaded in syringes and slowly pushed through FPADs. The filtrates were collected, and the filtration was repeated twice. Subsequently, 20 μL of the Ab2-GCDs reporter (1 mg/mL) was dropped on FPADs for labeling the captured sIgE. After 20 min, the unbounded Ab2-GCDs were removed by washing with PBS buffer for five times. To emphasize the efficiency of sIgE detection using the proposed method, the detection was performed by immersing FPADs in sIgE solutions as well. The immersion was proceeded for 0.5 h.

The selectivity test was performed by exposing FPADs to different components in blood, such as ions (use 200 mM of Na+ to mimic the total cations), nutrients (select 10 mM of glucose as the model interferent), amino acids (use 3 mM of histidine and 3 mM of glutamic acid to represent the positively-charged and negatively-charged species, respectively), and IgG (10 mg/mL). The analytes were dissolved in PBS buffer and processed following the same procedure in sIgE detection. To test their anti-interference capacity, the FPADs were exposed to sIgE (100 μg/mL) in the coexistence of the abovementioned interferents. The concentration of IgG in the anti-interference test varied from 10 mg/to 120 mg/mL to investigate the interference tolerance of sIgE detection.

All the fluorescence measurements were performed on an FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh instruments, UK) equipped with a liquid sample holder for the cuvette and a solid sample holder for FPADs. The excitation wavelength was fixed at 365 nm. The transmission electron microscope images of BCDs decorated cellulose fiber and GCDs were acquired on a JEM-2100 instrument (JEOL, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements were performed on an AXIS UltraDLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The JSM-7600F scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an X-ray Energy Dispersive Spectroscope (EDS) (Japan) was used to characterize the morphological and elemental information of the fluorescent paper and FPADs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Facile synthesis of the spectrally-resolved BCDs and GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FPAD was designed to detect Ara h2-specific IgE in a ratiometric fluorescence manner, which required two fluorophores with well-resolved emission profiles. Benefiting from the facilely adjustable emission properties of CDs, we synthesized BCDs on the surface of cellulose paper for providing the blue emission background of FPADs, and GCDs for labeling Ab2 and generating the sensing signal. Both CDs were synthesized using CA as the carbon source, whilst EDA and urea were used as nitrogen sources for BCDs and GCDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 A, on the defiberized cellulose, densely attached were the solid dots (BCDs) with an average diameter of 3.42 nm (Fig. S1 in the ESI), which represented the successful carbonization of CA and formation of BCDs in comparison to the cellulose from untreated paper. The High-resolution TEM image shows that the BCDs possessed apparent crystalline sp2 hybridized π-domain, consistent with graphite in lattice spacing (0.21 nm). The covalent bonding between cellulose substrate and BCDs contributed to the optical stability of fluorescent paper against drastic physical treatment. Negligible emission quenching was observed after two times of ultra-sonication (Fig. S2 in the ESI) For the mono-dispersed GCDs, the average diameter was 4.21 nm, and a sp2 hybridized π-domain was also observed (Fig. 1B). The XPS measurements declared the chemical properties of fluorescent paper and the GCDs. The appearance of the N1s signal on the cellulose paper verified the successful doping of nitrogen upon BCDs synthesis (Fig. S3 in the ESI). The deconvoluted C1s and N1s spectra indicated that the fluorescent paper was rich in carboxyl groups and amino groups, which could provide abundant reactive sites for assembling of Aha h2. Similar chemical properties were observed on the XPS data of GCDs (Fig. S4 in the ESI). The increment in the content of CC in comparison to the fluorescent paper was attributed to the absence of cellulose that lacked the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms.

The emission characteristics of the fluorescent paper and GCDs are shown in Fig. 1C. It was noticed that the maximum emissions of the fluorescent paper and GCDs centered at 450 nm and 526 nm, respectively. Moreover, both fluorescent paper and GCDs exhibited negligible excitation wavelength-dependent emissions, implying that BCDs and GCDs possessed uniform energy states. Ascribed to their well-resolved emissions, distinct emission peaks were shown on the spectra of the mixture of BCDs and GCDs (Fig. S5 in the ESI), confirming the possibility of ratiometric fluorescence output of FPADs. An excitation wavelength of 365 nm was selected for simultaneously irradiating the fluorescent paper and GCDs due to their excellent emission intensities at this excitation wavelength. In addition, the visualized quantification of sIgE was achievable using a hand-held UV lamp of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-functionalization of the fluorescent paper and GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sandwich-type assay of sIgE relied on the avid bindings of Ara h2/sIgE and sIgE/Ab2, in which the Ara h2 was immobilized on the fluorescent paper and Ab2 was conjugated to the GCDs. Both reactions were accomplished under the intermediate of EDC/NHS. For verifying the successful Ara h2 deposition, The SEM images, as well as the EDS mapping of the fluorescent paper were acquired. As shown in Fig. 2 A and Table S1, The C and O contents for the fluorescent paper with and without Ara h2 were close, nevertheless, increments in N and S contents were noticed for the paper carrying Ara h2. In fact, the bare cellulose paper contained almost no N due to the high cellulose purity. The appearance of N in the fluorescent paper originated from the successful synthesis of BCDs. The high N content and a certain concentration of S in Ara h2 were responsible for the N and S concentration increase in the Ara h2 functionalized fluorescent paper. The successful construction of Ab2-GCDs was proved by the FT-IR measurements. As shown in Fig. 2B, peaks on the spectrum of Ab2 indicated the existence of peptide linkage. The peaks at 1655 cm−1 and 1552 cm−1 represented the vibrations of the amide I band and II band, respectively. The amide III band at 1228 cm−1 was stemmed from the C–N stretching and N–H bending. The spectrum of GCDs reflected that the precursors (urea and citric acid) produced significant influences on the chemical properties of GCDs. Except for the amino group and amide linkage, the stretching of hydroxyl (3300 cm−1) and bending of C–O–C (1050 cm−1) are observed. After the modification of Ab2 by GCDs, the purified product exhibited the vibration peaks of both Ab2 and GCDs. This result can be direct evidence for the linkage of Ab2 and GCDs. As to the influence on the optical properties of GCDs after the linkage, we compared the emission profile of bare GCDs and Ab2-GCDs. There was no obvious emission quenching for Ab2-GCDs, implying the sufficient optical stability of GCDs (Fig. S6 in the ESI). The slight red shift was speculated due to the self-absorption effect among the GCDs particles on the surface of Ab2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficient and sensitive sIgE detection by harnessing the bi-functional FPADs
ce:section.ce:para:
The robust bio-conjugations of Ara h2 on fluorescent paper and GCDs on Ab2 permitted their application for sIgE detection. There were two possible resolutions for the detection procedure. One was the conventional immersion-based target capturing and labeling, which utilized the sensing function of FPADs. The other approach was a filtration-based assay that employed the filter and signal transduction functions of FPADs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, in the filtration-based assay, the emission color of FPADs gradually changed from blue to green, in the sIgE dose-dependent manner. The consistent blue-emissive FPAD in the absence of sIgE indicated that non-specific adsorption of Ab2-GCDs on the surface of FPAD was avoided due to the block effect of BSA. In the presence of sIgE, a sandwich-type Ara h2/sIgE/Ab2-GCDs complex was formed, causing a remarkable variation in emission color. The spectral data revealed the enhancement of the emission at 530 nm due to the attachment of Ab2-GCDs (Fig. S7 in the ESI). Quantitatively, the green-to-blue emission intensity ( I
530/ I
460) was linearly correlative to the logarithm of sIgE concentration (0.1–100 μg/mL). The regression equation for sIgE quantification was I
530/ I
460 = 0.39 log[sIgE] + 1.21. According to the 3δ criteria, the calculated limit of detection was 15.7 ng/mL, which was lower than the minimum sIgE concentration in human blood and comparable to the reported methods with outperforming sensitivities (Table S2 in the ESI). For the immersion-based assay (Fig. 3B), although the immersion time was as long as 0.5 h, the ratiometric fluorescence signal was remarkably weaker than that in the filtration-based assay upon adding a consistent amount of sIgE. Accordingly, an increase in the LOD by one magnitude (90.2 ng/mL) was obtained. The different target binding efficiency was responsible for the suppressed analytical performance of FPADs immersed in the analyte solution. The filtration effect provided by the cellulose paper substrate enabled the passing through all the liquid containing sIgE within a very short time. More importantly, the opportunity of sIgE encountering Ara h2 pre-assembled on FPADs was largely improved during the filtration.

Noticeably, the blue emission in FPADs remained unchanged in the entire sIgE detection procedure (Fig. S7). To figure out the contribution of blue emission to the analytical performances of FPADs, the non-fluorescent devices without BCDs were also constructed and used for sIgE quantification. Unlike the emission color change of FPADs, the non-fluorescent paper devices exhibited a gradually enhanced green emission along with the increased sIgE concentration (Fig. S8 in the ESI). It was found that quantification of sIgE can be achieved by harnessing the sIgE concentration-dependent (0.1–100 μg/mL) emission intensity of the paper sheets with a LOD of 16.3 ng/mL. Although similar sensitivities and a consistent dynamic detection range were revealed by both devices with or without a blue emission background, the reliability is considered to be better for BCDs decorated devices. For the single-emissive sensing platforms, the environmental factors, particularly the energy density of excitation sources, could impact the signal intensity, further interfering the analytical results. By inserting a consistent fluorescence signal background, the target abundances could be self-calibrated by the built-in fluorophore.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of sIgE in practical samples with excellent selectivity and accuracy
ce:section.ce:para:
The excellent analytical performance of the proposed bi-functional FPADS encouraged us to challenge the sIgE detection in practical samples. The sIgE with varying concentrations were spiked in serum and analyzed by FPADs following the identical procedure to that performed in PBS buffer. Prior to the assay, the selectivity and anti-interference capability of FPADs were examined by exchanging the sIgE with varying substances in the blood. As shown in Fig. 4 , the color change of FPADs exposed to high concentrations of salt, nutrients, and another type of antibody (IgE) was unobvious. In three parallel assays, the average values of I
530/ I
460 were smaller than 0.6, confirming that FPADs were capable of selectively responding to sIgE, but insensitive to other components in blood. Moreover, in the coexistence of the abovementioned substances, the emission color of FPADs that responded to sIgE (100 μg/mL) was negligibly changed relative to those exposed to sIgE only. The difference in I
530/ I
460 values was insignificant. Considering that the total protein concentration in serum is as high as 60 mg/mL, the amount of IgG, a kind of protein but also an IgE analogue, was further increased to investigate the interference tolerance of the proposed FPADs. The result showed that similar sIgE detection results were achieved in the coexistence of IgG less than 80 mg/mL (Fig. S9 in the ESI). The suppressed green emission of FPADs in the presence of sIgE and extremely high concentrations of IgG (80–120 mg/mL) was speculatively attributed to the blocking effect of dense protein molecules that forbidden the Ara h2/sIgE binding. These results demonstrated that the FPADs possessed excellent selectivity and were tolerant to the interference of other biological components, allowing the accurate quantification of sIgE in complex biological environments.

The sIgE assay in practical samples was conducted by spiking the serum with known concentrations of sIgE and analyzed by FPADs. The green-to-blue fluorescence ratio values were used to deduce the detected concentration of the sIgE. As shown in Fig. 5 , the average I
530/ I
460 values increased from 0.84 to 1.97 corresponding to the added sIgE amounts of 0.1–100 μg/mL. The estimated concentrations of sIgE were calculated according to the equation discussed in Section 3.3, and compared to the spiked sIgE amounts. As shown in Table 1 , the spike-recovery rate for sIgE was between 94 % and 115 % with the RSD values of less than 15 % in repeated assays. The recovery rate and accuracy of FPADs was close to the commercial testing kits, but advantageous in terms of cost efficiency and easy to use. These results evidenced that the proposed FPADs possessed promising prospects for quick clinical diagnosis and severity evaluation of peanut-caused allergy.

In summary, an innovative approach for the Ara h2-specific IgE assay method was exploited in our work. Benefited from its cost-efficiency and modification easiness, the cellulose-based filter paper was step-by-step functionalized in large scales to serve as analytical devices. The FPADs revealed desirable properties that met the requirements for clinical usage. The key findings in this work were enumerated as follows: 1) The sensitivity of FPADs was noticeably enhanced by combining their filtration function. The sIgE could be efficiently captured by Ara h2 upon the filtration; 2) The selectivity of FPADs was guaranteed by the avid bindings of Ara h2-sIgE and sIgE-Ab2; 3) The reliability of FPADs was satisfying based on the selectivity and the ratiometric fluorescent response. The blue emission background of the PADs provided a emission background to calibrate the detected emission signal, and exhibited the distinct emission color change; 4) The rapidness of sIgE detection was realized by taking the advantage of FPADs. In this assay, the filtration and labeling procedures could be accomplished within 30 min. As a ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform, the FPADs are competitive among allergy-related biomarker detections. In addition, we believe that the FPADs for varying targets can be achieved by flexibly engineering the capture moieties on the surface of the fluorescent paper.

Ze-Nan Ma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jun-Jie Ding: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xin-Qiao Shi: Visualization, Investigation. Ying Yuan: Validation, Formal analysis. Meng-Tian Wang: Visualization, Investigation. Li-Na Yu: Visualization, Software. Xiao-Jun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Peng Shen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the important financial support from Suzhou Wuzhong People's Hospital.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2025.343922.
